currency.name: Swiss Franc
currency.budgetNote: Recommended: 30 CHF (about £27). Switzerland is one of the priciest countries in the world.

mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Switzerland on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe — landlocked in the middle, no coastline. 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Switzerland?
mapQuestions.answer: Bern. Most people guess Zürich or Geneva, but the capital is actually Bern.

mapQuestions.question: How many countries border Switzerland?
mapQuestions.answer: Five: Germany, France, Italy, Austria, and Liechtenstein.

mapQuestions.question: How many official languages does Switzerland have?
mapQuestions.answer: Four: German, French, Italian, and Romansh.

mapQuestions.question: What's Switzerland's tallest mountain — and why is the Matterhorn so famous?
mapQuestions.answer: Tallest is Monte Rosa (4,634 m). The Matterhorn (4,478 m) is famous for its perfect pyramid shape — and the Toblerone chocolate is shaped like it.

foods.name: Fondue
foods.description: Pot of melted cheese (Gruyère + Emmental + white wine). Dip cubes of bread on long forks. Don't drop yours — Swiss tradition says you have to do a dare.

foods.name: Rösti
foods.description: Crispy fried grated potato cake. Originally a farmer's breakfast, now eaten any time.

foods.name: Swiss chocolate
foods.description: The Swiss invented milk chocolate in 1875. Average Swiss person eats 11 kg of chocolate per year.

foods.name: Raclette
foods.description: Half a wheel of cheese melted under a grill, scraped onto potatoes, gherkins, and pickled onions.

foods.name: Bürli
foods.description: Crusty bread rolls — Switzerland has hundreds of regional bread varieties.

foods.name: Birchermüesli
foods.description: Oats soaked in milk with apple, lemon juice, and nuts. Invented by a Swiss doctor in 1900 for hospital patients.

scavengerItems.label: A cow wearing a giant bell
A red train climbing up a mountain
A snow-capped peak from a town square
An edelweiss flower (real or on something Swiss-souvenir)
An old town fountain with drinkable water
A bicycle parked unlocked outside a shop
A traditional clockmaker or watch shop window
A bakery with at least 5 different bread types
A cable car going up into the clouds
A Swiss flag flying from a private home
A perfectly stacked woodpile outside a chalet
A Swiss army knife in a shop window

geoMatches.description: The biggest city — banks, lakes, trendy restaurants, and a beautiful old town centre.
Snow-capped peaks across the south. Skiing in winter, hiking and lake swimming in summer.
Huge curving lake on the French border. Lausanne, Geneva, and Montreux all sit on it.
A picture-perfect old town on a lake, with a famous wooden covered bridge from 1333.
A car-free mountain village right under the Matterhorn. Reachable only by train. Famous for skiing and cheese.

senses.smell: Fresh alpine air, melted cheese, wood smoke from chalets, pine trees, hot chocolate…
senses.hear: Cowbells ringing across a valley, train horns echoing in tunnels, "Grüezi!" from strangers…
senses.taste: Stringy melted cheese, dark chocolate, crisp mountain water, crusty fresh bread, salty pickles…
senses.feel: Cold mountain water on your hands, soft snow, smooth wood of an old chalet, sharp alpine air…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why hasn't Switzerland been in a war for over 200 years?
stories.body: Switzerland is officially "neutral" — it doesn't take sides in wars. The country last fought in 1815, over 200 years ago. Through both World Wars, Swiss neutrality meant Switzerland stayed safe while neighbours were devastated. Switzerland still has a serious army though — every Swiss man does military training, and the country has hidden bunkers in mountains that could shelter the whole population. The neutrality lets them host the International Red Cross (founded by a Swiss man, Henri Dunant) and many UN agencies in Geneva.

stories.question: Why are there cows wearing huge bells?
stories.body: Swiss farmers have herded cattle up to high alpine pastures every summer for over 1000 years, then back down to the valley before winter. The huge bells — sometimes 5kg each — help farmers find cows in the mountain mist. When the cows come back down in autumn (the "Alpabzug"), they're decorated with flowers and giant bells, and villages have parades. The biggest bell goes on the cow that produced the most milk that summer.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A "big bell goes to the best worker" prize. What's something at home or school you'd give a big-bell prize for?

stories.question: What's the world's biggest particle smasher and why is it under Switzerland?
stories.body: Underneath Geneva, on the French border, there's a 27 km circular tunnel called the Large Hadron Collider (LHC). Scientists from all over the world fire particles around it at nearly the speed of light to smash them together — that's how they discovered the Higgs boson in 2012, the particle that gives everything else mass. The LHC is the biggest scientific instrument ever built. Right above it people are walking their dogs and drinking coffee, with no idea what's happening 100m below their feet.

stories.question: How did chocolate become Swiss?
stories.body: Cocoa beans come from South America, but Switzerland has a 200-year head start on perfecting chocolate. In 1875 Daniel Peter (a Swiss man) invented MILK chocolate by combining cocoa with the dried milk a neighbour, Henri Nestlé, had been working on. A year later Rodolphe Lindt invented "conching" — stirring chocolate for hours until it's silky smooth. Brands like Lindt, Toblerone, and Nestlé are all Swiss. The average Swiss person eats 11 kg of chocolate a year — that's about 200 chocolate bars.

stories.question: Why does Switzerland have four official languages?
stories.body: Switzerland was never conquered into being one country — it formed in 1291 when three small valley communities made a pact to defend each other against the Habsburgs. Over centuries more regions joined, each keeping its own language. Today: about 63% speak German (a Swiss-German dialect very different from "real" German), 23% speak French, 8% Italian, and less than 1% speak Romansh (a Latin-based language only found in eastern Switzerland). Most Swiss children learn at least two of these at school.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Switzerland has stayed peaceful with four languages and many cultures. Why do you think some other countries with similar mixes fight, but Switzerland doesn't?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Switzerland so far?
Mountains or lakes — which Swiss landscape do you like better?
Swiss things are famous for being precise (watches, trains, chocolate). Do you like things to be precise or relaxed?
Switzerland is one of the most expensive countries in the world. What's the most surprising price you've seen?
Which Swiss language would you most want to learn — German, French, Italian, or Romansh?
Switzerland has been neutral for 200+ years. Is that a strength or a weakness?

wordSearch.hint: Swiss foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Alpine cow with bell
animals.description: Brown Swiss cows wearing giant bells, herded up to high pastures every summer.

animals.name: Golden eagle
animals.description: Above the high peaks — about 350 breeding pairs in the Swiss Alps.

animals.name: Alpine ibex
animals.description: Sure-footed wild goats with curved horns, on the steepest cliff faces.

animals.name: Alpine marmot
animals.description: Burrowing rodents — listen for their sharp warning whistle on alpine trails.

animals.name: Red fox
animals.description: Common in cities AND mountains. Often spotted at dusk in Zurich and Bern parks.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: Returning to eastern Switzerland from Italy after 100+ years gone. Very rare sighting.

animals.name: Wolf pack
animals.description: About 30 wild wolf packs in Switzerland — controversial as they prey on sheep.
